texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions cause signi fi cant impacts on the environment and must therefore be controlled even more stringently. This requires the development of cost-e ff ective removal strategies which simultaneously create value-added by-products or energy from the waste. This study aims to treat gaseous nitric oxide (NO) by hollow- fi bre membrane bio fi lm reactor (HFMBfR) in the presence of nitrite (NO -2 ) and evaluate nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions formed as an intermediate product during the denitri fi cation process. Accumulated N2O can be utilised in methane oxidation as an oxidant to produce energy. In the fi rst stage of the study, the HFMBfR was operated by feeding only gaseous NO as the nitrogen source. During this period, the best performance was achieved with 92% NO removal e ffi ciency (RE). In the second stage, both NO gas and NO -2 were supplied to the system, and 91% NO and 99% NO -2 reduction were achieved simultaneously with the maximum N2O generation of 386 ± 31 ppm. Lower in fl uent carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratios, such as 4.5 and 2.0, and higher NO -2 -N loading rate of 158 mg N day -1 favoured N2O generation. An improved NO removal rate and N2O accumulation were seen with the increasing amount of PO 3 -4 in the medium. The 16S rDNA sequencing analysis revealed that Alicycliphilus denitri fi cans and Pseudomonas putida were the dominant species. The study shows that an HFMBfR can be successfully used to eliminate both NO -2 and gaseous NO and simultaneously generate N2O by adjusting the system parameters such as C/N ratio, NO -2 and PO 3 -4 loading.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the last two decades, biological NOx control technologies have emerged as an alternative to conventional energy-intensive reduction processes such as selective catalytic reduction (SCR) and selective noncatalytic reduction (SNCR) which require high capital and operating costs [5, 6]. Primarily, traditional bio fi lters and biotrickling fi lters have been used for NOx removal, in which bio fi lm forms on the packing media and waste gas is fi ltered through the media. However, poor water solubility of NO limits the biodegradation capacity of the microorganisms in these bio fi ltration systems and necessitates long contact times [7 -17]. In order to enhance removal e ffi ciency (RE) of NO, various bioreactor con fi gurations such as BioDeNOx [18, 19], jet-loop bioreactor (JLBR) [20], and hollow- fi bre membrane bio fi lm reactor (HFMBfR) [21 -30] have been developed subsequently. HFMBfR is one of the most suitable bioreactors for the treatment of low-soluble gases such as NO, thanks to its compact design with supporting bacteria growth over the membrane and providing a large mass transfer area between gaseous pollutant and liquid. Its unique bubble-free gas delivery to the bio fi lm attached to the membrane surface improves treatment capacity and scales down energy consumption [30].

HFMBfR is one of the most suitable bioreactors for the treatment of low-soluble gases such as NO, thanks to its compact design with supporting bacteria growth over the membrane and providing a large mass transfer area between gaseous pollutant and liquid. Its unique bubble-free gas delivery to the bio fi lm attached to the membrane surface improves treatment capacity and scales down energy consumption [30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To date, only a limited number of studies have investigated biological NO removal via HFMBfR. Membrane bio fi lm reactor for NO removal has been examined based on biological processes including nitri fi cation [21], denitri fi cation [22] and simultaneous nitri fi cation/ denitri fi cation [24, 25]. Biological denitri fi cation is capable of reducing NO to inert nitrogen gas (N2) under anoxic conditions, while nitri fi cation is an aerobic process converting NO to nitrate (NO -3 ) as a fi nal product which requires further treatment to be disposed of. In order to make HFMBfR technology more cost e ff ective, various strategies have also been explored. A study reported that high in fl uent NO -3 loading to the HFMBfR enhances the NO reduction by stimulating denitri fi ers activity [26]. The feasibility of simultaneous NO reduction and mercury oxidation in an HFMBfR was also demonstrated [27 -29]. In a recent study, the e ff ect of gas fl ow conditions on NO removal in HFMBfR was investigated and suggested the operating HFMBfR in alternating fl ow-through and dead-end gas supplying modes to improve the elimination capacity (EC) without compromising RE [30]. However, to our knowledge, none of these studies have evaluated N2O generation performance of the system.

texts.orig: times greater than CO2), but it can be also utilised as an oxidant to produce energy in combustion engines in racing vehicles and in aerospace industries [31, 32]. Using N2O as the oxygen source can generate 37% more energy than using O2 in methane combustion [33 -38]. Scherson et al. [34] found that the energy production capacity increased by 5.7 -7.3% when N2O and O2 are added to the biogas combustion reactor [34]. Typically, complete denitri fi cation consists of four reduction steps (NO -3 → NO -2 → NO → N2O → N2) while partial denitri fi cation of NO -3 , NO -2 or NO to N2O requires fewer reduction steps. Thus, NO reduction with one step to N2O may be a promising tool for both removing of NO from waste gas streams and producing N2O to be used in energy generation. It has also the advantage of creating less CO2, reducing sludge generation, and increasing denitri fi cation rate up to 1.5 -2 times [39]. Yu et al. [40] achieved 90% nitrogen RE with 70% N2O conversion when NO (420 -700 mg N L -1 ) was partially denitri fi ed to N2O in an up fl ow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor [41], while Myung et al. [35] reduced NO -2 to N2O obtaining 72% nitrogen reduction with 70% N2O conversion during 120 days [35]. Hence, upgraded biological nitrogen elimination can be achieved more economically and environmentally friendly via partial denitri fi cation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several studies investigated N2O generation from wastewater based on nitrogen removal process such as coupled aerobic -anoxic nitrous decomposition operation (CANDO) which is a two-stage process: (1) nitritation of ammonia (NH + 4 ) to NO -2 ; (2) partial denitri fi cation of NO -2 to N2O [33 -36]. In lower chemical oxygen demand (COD) to nitrogen (COD/N) ratio, limited availability of electron donors resulted in nitrite accumulation which has inhibitory e ff ect on N2O reduction [42]. In a recent study, up to 97.5% N2O conversion from nitrite-containing wastewater was achieved with lowering the pH under strictly anaerobic conditions [43]. In a heterotrophic denitri fi cation, N 2 O reduction is de fi nitely sensitive to dissolved oxygen concentration [44]. Chen et al. [44] reported free nitrous acid (FNA) accumulation is positively correlated with N2O emissions [40]. In a recent study, it was found out that high levels of NO can inhibit nitrous oxide reductase resulting in N2O accumulation during the biological NO reduction processes [41].

texts.orig: 2. Experimental design

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Description of HFMBfR system
texts.text: 2.1. Description of HFMBfR system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lab-scale HFMBfR is shown schematically in Figure 1. The reactor consists of 128 non-porous polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) hollow- fi bre membranes with an active length of 50 cm. The speci fi c membrane surface area is 0.1 m 2 in which the inner and outer diameter of the PDMS membrane (OxyMem, Ireland) are 0.3 and 0.5 mm, respectively. The PDMS membrane has been reported as a suitable material for the biological removal of the waste gas stream in terms of easy fabrication, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and biodurability [45]. The volume of the reactor is 1 L, including tubings and a sampling bottle. The nutrient medium was kept in the 15 L bottle, sterilised at 121°C in an autoclave, to eliminate contamination. As shown in Figure 1, two pumps were used, one for feeding fresh nutrients and one for recirculating liquid medium in the reactor to enhance the bio fi lm formation on the membrane surface Besides, pressure sensors to control internal membrane gas pressure, gas inlet and outlet ports, pH controller, and silica column to eliminate humidity between the gas outlet and analyser were installed as well. The pH of the medium was kept at 7 throughout the study by supplying hydrochloric (HCl) acid or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) base solution to the system.

The speci fi c membrane surface area is 0.1 m 2 in which the inner and outer diameter of the PDMS membrane (OxyMem, Ireland) are 0.3 and 0.5 mm, respectively. The volume of the reactor is 1 L, including tubings and a sampling bottle. The nutrient medium was kept in the 15 L bottle, sterilised at 121°C in an autoclave, to eliminate contamination. As shown in Figure 1, two pumps were used, one for feeding fresh nutrients and one for recirculating liquid medium in the reactor to enhance the bio fi lm formation on the membrane surface Besides, pressure sensors to control internal membrane gas pressure, gas inlet and outlet ports, pH controller, and silica column to eliminate humidity between the gas outlet and analyser were installed as well. The pH of the medium was kept at 7 throughout the study by supplying hydrochloric (HCl) acid or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) base solution to the system.

texts.orig: 2.2. Denitrifying medium
texts.text: 2.2. Denitrifying medium

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Activated sludge was taken from an advanced wastewater treatment plant located in Kocaeli, Turkey. The sludge was then inoculated into an autoclave bottle containing nutrient medium, which consists of sodium acetate (Na·CH3COO), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) and 1 mL L -1 of trace elements; CaCl2 -2H2O = 2000 mg L -1 , MnSO4 -7H2O = 500 mg L -1 , FeSO4 -7H2O = 1000 mg L -1 , ZnSO4 -7H2O = 200 mg L -1 , CuSO4 -5H2O = 100 mg L -1 , CoCl2 -6H2O = 100 mg L -1 . NO -2 -N concentration ranged from 10 mg L -1 -40 mg L -1 with a hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 6 -18 h while inlet NO -N was provided as (0.3 -1.2 mg L -1 ) with gas retention time (GRT) (within membrane module) of 11 -54 s. The carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio varied from 2 to 37.5, while phosphate (PO 3 -4 ) concentration ranged between 5 and 15 mmol L -1 . Biomass acclimation was conducted for 90 days in a shaker incubator at 180 rpm and room temperature.

texts.orig: Liquid in fl uent and e ffl uent samples were collected every day and analysed for COD and NO -2 concentrations. All samples were fi ltered through 0.45 μ m syringe fi lter before the analysis. COD and nitrite concentrations were determined using 5220(C) closed titrimetric re fl ux method and 4500-NO -2 B colorimetric method as described in the standard methods, respectively [46]. A pH controller was integrated into the system to keep the pH at 7. Chemiluminescence NOx analyser (Model 42i-HL, Thermo Fisher Scienti fi c Inc., U.S.A.) monitored inlet and outlet concentrations of NO gas. N2O generations were evaluated through a D12 gas transmitter with an infrared sensor (ATI, P/N 00-1431, Analytical Technology Inc., U.S.A.) that was incorporated into the reactor control system providing continuous measurement by a 60-s interval.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. NO removal performance of HFMBfR
texts.text: 3.1. NO removal performance of HFMBfR

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The HFMBfR was investigated for its performance of NO reduction and EC initially by six phases of operation, as shown in Figure 2. NO RE of the HFMBfR was achieved between 38% and 97% under di ff erent operating conditions. In the fi rst three phases (Phase 1 -3), the e ff ect of GFR on NO removal was evaluated. During the second three phases (Phase 4 -6), the system was operated at varying MPs. After this fi rst stage of feeding the system with NO gas as the sole nitrogen source, it was focused on the e ff ect of feeding medium composition on the system performance. For the next eight phases (Phase 7 -14) various concentrations of NO -2 , C/N ratio, and PO 3 -4 in the feeding medium were introduced to the system and N2O emissions were also monitored.

After this fi rst stage of feeding the system with NO gas as the sole nitrogen source, it was focused on the e ff ect of feeding medium composition on the system performance. For the next eight phases (Phase 7 -14) various concentrations of NO -2 , C/N ratio, and PO 3 -4 in the feeding medium were introduced to the system and N2O emissions were also monitored.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The GFR, which is inversely related to GRT, was one of the major factors which dramatically a ff ected NO RE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and NO EC of the HFMBfR. Higher RE was obtained at a lower GFR. As shown in Figure 3, the highest NO RE of 97% was achieved at the minimum GFR of 5 mL min -1 in Phase 1 while the lowest RE of 43% was observed during Phase 3 with the maximum fl ow rate of 25 mL min -1 . During Phase 2, a moderate NO RE of 86% of NO and an EC of 41 mg NO m -2 d -1 were attained with a GFR of 10 mL min -1 . Observed fl uctuations in this phase were caused by some operational problems, such as gas leakage and pump failure. Overall, we saw that higher GFR enhances NO EC while it reduces NO RE by shortening the gas residence time in the membrane lumen which can be seen particularly in Phase 3 with accompanying lower removal rates. NO EC, on the other hand, signi fi cantly increases with increasing GFR since more gas per unit area and time is supplied. The largest EC of 72.8 mg NO m -2 d -1 was obtained at the highest fl ow rate of 25 mL min -1 in Phase 3. In previous studies by Min et al. [21] and Zhang et al. [22], greater NO elimination capacities were reported. However, in the study by Min et al. [21], NO gas was converted to nitrate by bio fi lm nitri fi cation, which is known to be a much faster reaction than denitri fi cation. On the other hand, Zhang et al. [22] operated the reactor with a closed-end membrane module to force all the gas transferred into the bio fi lm, resulting in the enhancement of the capacity.

texts.orig: The performance of the HFMBfR was slightly improved in Phase 5, accompanying 59% of the average gas RE and 132 mg NO m -2 d -1 of EC during two weeks of observation by extending the pressure to 20 kPa. In Phase 6, no gas pressure was applied. There was a signi fi cant di ff erence between Phase 5 and Phase 6 in terms of both NO removal rate and EC. The NO RE dramatically declined to its half at the earlier period of Phase 6. Then the reactor exhibited a response with an increasing trend of both NO removal rate and EC in subsequent 14 days of operation. Average stable response for NO removal rate and EC were 38% and 84 mg NO m -2 d -1 , respectively for this phase. Therefore, applying pressure to the membrane bio fi lm results in the better performance of the HFMBfR.

texts.orig: Inlet NO concentration showed a slim e ff ect on gas RE with approximately ±10% di ff erence. However, in terms of NO EC, inlet NO in fl uenced the EC greatly; the capacity increased 1.5 times with the highest inlet NO concentration. It could be explained that the higher inlet NO concentration provided biomass-enriched bio fi lm with enough nutrient source and improved bacterial growth, providing the capability to eliminate more NO [12, 48].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. N2O generation
texts.text: 3.2. N2O generation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considering the parameters that can improve the treatment capacity according to the experience of the previous phases, the remaining phases (from Phase 7 to Phase 14) were operated for the evaluation of both NO removal and N2O generation and carried out in the presence of nitrite. N2Ogenerations were monitored by a 60s interval starting from Phase 7. During the next eight phases, the system response was analysed, focusing on di ff erent NO -2 content, C/N ratio, and PO 3 -4 concentration in the liquid feeding medium. Daily mean N2O gas emissions ranged from 70 ± 19 ppm to 494 ± 36 ppm within 130 days of the experimental period. Phase 14 achieved the highest emission of N2O (386 ± 31 ppm) alongside high NO RE of 91%.
texts.text: Considering the parameters that can improve the treatment capacity according to the experience of the previous phases, the remaining phases (from Phase 7 to Phase 14) were operated for the evaluation of both NO removal and N2O generation and carried out in the presence of nitrite. N2Ogenerations were monitored by a 60s interval starting from Phase 7. During the next eight phases, the system response was analysed, focusing on di ff erent NO -2 content, C/N ratio, and PO 3 -4 concentration in the liquid feeding medium. Daily mean N2O gas emissions ranged from 70 ± 19 ppm to 494 ± 36 ppm within 130 days of the experimental period. Phase 14 achieved the highest emission of N2O (386 ± 31 ppm) alongside high NO RE of 91%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.1. E ff ect of NO -2 loading
texts.text: 3.2.1. E ff ect of NO -2 loading

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NO -2 feeding played an important role in N2O production in the system. Three phases were operated with di ff erent NO -2 -N loading rates (53.3, 158.4, and 79.2 mg N day -1 ), as shown in Figure 5(a). 110 ± 20 ppm of N2O was observed during Phase 7, together with a 48% NO reduction with NO -2 supply of 53.3 mg N day -1 for 15 days. N2O emission was steadily increased and reached its highest value on day 9 with 135 ± 20 ppm, then decreased gradually. In Phase 8, N2O generation jumped to 319 ± 59 ppm while NO RE dropped to the lowest value of 32% by feeding excessive NO -2 (158.4 mg N day -1 ) to the reactor. N2O emission fi rst reached 429 ± 61 ppm within the initial 6 days of the phase and then the reactor remained stable despite the fl uctuations in the beginning. These fl uctuations might be caused by the sharp rise in NO -2 . Shortening HRT can a ff ect microorganisms in handling additional NO -2 load and treatment e ffi ciency can be lower due to this sudden overload. After the reactor regained its stability in Phase 8, NO -2 feeding was reduced to half (79.2 mg N day -1 ) in Phase 9. NO reduction was increased to 50% in this phase and N2O gas emissions were gently improved up to 155 ± 8 ppm after 6 days of operation.

N2O emission was steadily increased and reached its highest value on day 9 with 135 ± 20 ppm, then decreased gradually. In Phase 8, N2O generation jumped to 319 ± 59 ppm while NO RE dropped to the lowest value of 32% by feeding excessive NO -2 (158.4 mg N day -1 ) to the reactor. N2O emission fi rst reached 429 ± 61 ppm within the initial 6 days of the phase and then the reactor remained stable despite the fl uctuations in the beginning. These fl uctuations might be caused by the sharp rise in NO -2 . Shortening HRT can a ff ect microorganisms in handling additional NO -2 load and treatment e ffi ciency can be lower due to this sudden overload. After the reactor regained its stability in Phase 8, NO -2 feeding was reduced to half (79.2 mg N day -1 ) in Phase 9. NO reduction was increased to 50% in this phase and N2O gas emissions were gently improved up to 155 ± 8 ppm after 6 days of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results illustrated that addition of higher NO -2 amount in fresh medium soared N2O emissions. Our results are also consistent with the previous fi ndings concluded that change in NO -2 concentration strongly in fl uences N2O generation with an upward trend [49, 50]. Alinsa fi et al. [49] reported that N2O conversion rate increased from 2.81 -11.29% when NO -2 addition was raised from 10 mg to 20 mg N L -1 during denitri fi cation in a batch reactor. The study con fi rmed the inhibitory e ff ect of NO -2 concentration on N2O reduction, mentioning NO -2 and NO inhibited the microbial growth because of their high a ffi nity for metal ions located at the active site of enzymes [49, 51]. Probably, the inhibitory e ff ect may not directly due to NO -2 , but to its role in the biological formation of NO, which is a more toxic compound [52]. The inhibition of N2O reduction by nitrous acid (HNO2) has also been demonstrated as a result of pH decrease [53]. Sun et al. [50] observed that increasing NO -2 concentrations (1 -10 mmol L -1 ) under variable or fi xed C/N ratios led to more partitioning to N2O (0 -0.55 mmol L -1 ) which may be a direct e ff ect of NO -2 detoxi fi cation [50]. Feeding high concentrations of nitrite to the reactor decreases the NO RE while it improves N2O generation simultaneously. Typically, the denitri fi cation process produces CO2 as a byproduct that can possibly a ff ect NO RE by decreasing the denitrifying enzyme activity (DEA) [54]. Therefore, it should be noted that if the aim is to enhance N2O generation together with NO removal, the amount of nitrite supplied becomes a critical parameter and needs to be adjusted accordingly.

texts.text: 3.2.3. E ff ect of PO ₄ ³ -concentration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The HFMBfR was monitored for di ff erent PO 3 -4 concentration of feeding medium during Phase 11, 13, and 14 (Figure 5(c)). PO 3 -4 feeding was increased from 5 mmol L -1 (Phase 11) to 8 mmol L -1 in Phase 13 that was operated for 15 days. During this phase, the average N2O emission of 334 ± 34 ppm was obtained along with 89% of NO RE. Subsequently, in Phase 14, the reactor system reached its highest performance in terms of N2O production; 386 ± 31 ppm of N2O generation was observed with 15 mmol L -1 of PO 3 -4 supply. In other words, increasing the phosphate concentration to three times increased N2O production 1.5 times. The NO RE for all these three phases was around 90% suggesting that PO 3 -4 concentrations in this range do not a ff ect the NO reduction.
texts.text: The HFMBfR was monitored for di ff erent PO 3 -4 concentration of feeding medium during Phase 11, 13, and 14 (Figure 5(c)). PO 3 -4 feeding was increased from 5 mmol L -1 (Phase 11) to 8 mmol L -1 in Phase 13 that was operated for 15 days. During this phase, the average N2O emission of 334 ± 34 ppm was obtained along with 89% of NO RE. Subsequently, in Phase 14, the reactor system reached its highest performance in terms of N2O production; 386 ± 31 ppm of N2O generation was observed with 15 mmol L -1 of PO 3 -4 supply. In other words, increasing the phosphate concentration to three times increased N2O production 1.5 times. The NO RE for all these three phases was around 90% suggesting that PO 3 -4 concentrations in this range do not a ff ect the NO reduction.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is typically one of the denitrifying bacteria that is frequently enriched in wastewater treatment plants and bioreactors. It is genetically tractable and can survive when the nosZ gene is deleted from its chromosome [58]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from these factors discussed above, the inlet NO concentration can also be in fl uencing N2O generation. Compared to the study by Yu et al. [40], which achieved a 70% N2O conversion rate by denitrifying 420 -700 mg N L -1 of in fl uent NO, this study achieved a lower e ffi ciency (17 ± 1%) for 0.3 -1.2 mg N L -1 of in fl uent NO (Table 2). Other mechanisms, such as the type of carbon source, nutrient-feeding regime, and di ff erent microorganisms can a ff ect N2O generation as well [41, 59]. Zhao et al. [37] reported higher N2O conversion e ffi ciency when glucose was supplied as a carbon source to sequencing batch reactor (SBR) rather than feeding by methanol, ethanol, and sodium acetate [37]. Besides, the feeding scheme of the nutrients plays a vital role in N2O generation. As reported by Scherson et al. [33], there was no emission of N2O from a wellmixed reactor by continuous feeding of acetate and nitrite together. 9 -12% of N2O conversion e ffi ciency was achieved when coupled-nutrients were provided as pulses, whereas it dramatically improved to 60 -65% when decoupled-feeding of acetate and nitrite were introduced [33]. It is recommended for future studies that enhancement of N2O production could be engineered by investigating other parameters such as temperature, pH, the CO2 content of inlet gas, alternative

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The metagenomics analysis of the bio fi lm was performed to identify its microbial community structure at the end of the bioreactor operation. As shown in Figure 6, phylum was dominated by Proteobacteria (96.53%), and only few sequences belonged to Actinobacteria (2.5%), Bacteroidetes (0.12%), Planctomycetes (0.11%), and Firmicutes (0.11%). These results were consistent with the previous studies, which reported that denitrifying bacteria were generally from the Proteobacteria , Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes [60, 61]. The dominant bacteria genus in the HFMBfR such as Alicycliphilus (53.18%), Pseudomonas (9.22%), Bradyrhizobium (3.75%), Sphingomonas (2.61%), Stenotrophomonas (2.07%), Delftia (1.72%), Acidovorax (1.6%), Comamonas (1.37%), Ralstonia (1.35%), Dechloromonas (1.15%) were identi fi ed. Alicycliphilus denitri fi cans (53.18%) and Pseudomonas putida (6.2%) were the most abundant bacteria species in the reactor.

Whereas, the nosZ genes of the denitri fi ers can be inhibited by high phosphate supply [57] which was actually observed in the phases of high phosphate feedings. Pseudomonas putida has a complicated metabolic process and the potential to treat nitrogen-polluted wastewater at low temperatures [64]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Bradyrhizobium is a Gram-negative bacterium which fi xes nitrogen, but its denitrifying pathways can be alternative if the oxygen levels decline. While Bradyrhizobium diazoe ffi ciens can completely denitrify nitrate to N2, Bradyrhizobium japonicum partially denitri fi es nitrate and is unable to reduce N2O to N2 under anaerobic conditions [65]. Interestingly, Sphingomonas , usually identi fi ed as absolute aerobes, were seen in the HFMBfR. Although they were only about 2%, probably they could survive since the system was not strictly anaerobic, and also might get contamination during the preparation steps. Otherwise, a study con fi rmed that Sphingomonas wittichii RW1 reduced NO -2 to N2O encoding nitrite and NO reductases, and thus could be classi fi ed as denitri fi ers [66] while some Sphingomonas strains were negative for reduction of NO -3 to NO -2 [67]. Other species such as Stenotrophomonas , Delftia , Acidovorax , Comamonas , Ralstonia , Dechloromonas usually involve as heterotrophic denitri fi ers in biological nitrogen removal processes [33, 34, 38, 59].

texts.orig: Microbial community structures, which consist of actively interacting and competing microorganisms, are typically complex and dynamic [68]. They may di ff er under the long-term investigation with di ff erent operational modes. The previous studies reported that biodegradability of carbon sources, temperature, pH, C/N ratio, nitrite concentration and DO concentration were the main factors in fl uencing denitrifying bacteria growth [69, 70]. Adjusting the nutrient supply rate and the above-in fl uenced parameters may control over the microbial enrichment reasonably for NO reduction that may be a common property of denitrifying bacteria [63] which accounted as 97% in this analysis.

texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Performance of HFMBfR on NO gas removal was investigated for di ff erent operating parameters such as GFR, MP, inlet NO load. The system can achieve 89 -92% NO removal rate and (196 -204) mg NO m -2 d -1 of EC coupled with (250 -400) ppm of average N2O to be used for energy generation. GFR and MP were the most in fl uenced factors to perform NO reduction. PDMS membrane used in the HFMBfR showed excellent performance within the conditions tried in the system. They could resist the high gas pressure and high liquid fl ow rate, no leakage problem once built into a robust bundle, supported bio fi lm formation e ff ectively.
texts.text: Performance of HFMBfR on NO gas removal was investigated for di ff erent operating parameters such as GFR, MP, inlet NO load. The system can achieve 89 -92% NO removal rate and (196 -204) mg NO m -2 d -1 of EC coupled with (250 -400) ppm of average N2O to be used for energy generation. GFR and MP were the most in fl uenced factors to perform NO reduction. PDMS membrane used in the HFMBfR showed excellent performance within the conditions tried in the system. They could resist the high gas pressure and high liquid fl ow rate, no leakage problem once built into a robust bundle, supported bio fi lm formation e ff ectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NO -2 feeding played a pivotal role in producing more N2O, yet the reactor stability and response of microorganisms should be considered alternatively. It was observed that a lower in fl uent C/N ratio and higher PO 3 -4 concentration increase N2O generation. The system ' s best performance in terms of N2O generation was observed in Phase 14; 91% NO removal, 99% nitrite reduction and maximum N2O generation of 386 ± 31 ppm (8 ± 0.6 mg N day -1 ) were achieved (Table 3). Alicycliphilus denitri fi cans and Pseudomonas putida were the dominant species reducing NO in the reactor.

texts.orig: Using wastewater containing NO -2 or NO -3 as the feeding to the reactor, N2O generation can be enhanced, and this hybrid system can be considered a way to explore the concept of ' from waste gas and wastewater to energy ' .
